1: Jotham was 25 and reigned 16 years in Jerusalem. His mother was Jerushah daughter of Zadok — a priestly family name, significant in a chapter about a king who respects priestly prerogatives. The priest-family mother of the faithful son is the Chronicler's genealogical commentary. Christ is born of priestly and royal lineage combined: Mary of the tribe of Levi (Elizabeth her relative was of Aaron, Luke 1:36), and the Davidic legal line through Joseph.
2: Jotham did what was right in the eyes of YHWH according to all his father Uzziah had done — but unlike him he did not enter the temple of YHWH. Jotham's positive characteristic is a restraint: he did not repeat his father's sin. The negative clause ('did not enter the temple') becomes the positive statement of his reign. Christ's faithfulness is also characterized in part by what he refused: he 'was in every respect tempted as we are, yet without sin' (Hebrews 4:15). The without is constitutive of the qualification for the priesthood he holds.
3: Jotham built the upper gate of the house of YHWH; he built extensively on the wall of Ophel; he built cities in the Judean hills and forts and towers in the wooded areas. The building program of the faithful king — investing in infrastructure without grasping priestly prerogatives. 'Unless the LORD builds the house, those who build it labor in vain' (Psalms 127:1); Christ is the master builder whose building program is the church he raises (Matthew 16:18).
4: Jotham built cities in the hill country of Judah and forts and towers in the wooded hills. The expansion of secure settlement into previously undeveloped territory. Christ's mission also reaches into previously unoccupied spiritual territory: 'I have been ambitious to preach the gospel, not where Christ has already been named, lest I build on someone else's foundation' (Romans 15:20) — the mission as frontier-settlement.
5: Jotham fought the king of the Ammonites and prevailed; the Ammonites gave him 100 talents of silver and 10,000 kors of wheat and barley for three years. The tribute-payment from Ammon acknowledges Jotham's covenantal authority in the region. Christ does not receive tribute in material goods but receives the tribute of worship: 'the nations will walk by its light, and the kings of the earth will bring their glory into it' (Revelation 21:24) — the eschatological tribute that all earthly tribute types.
6: 'Jotham became mighty because he ordered his ways before YHWH his God.' The causal chain: ordered covenant life → divine strengthening. Christ's own life demonstrates this principle from the inside: 'I do as the Father has commanded me' (John 14:31); 'he learned obedience through what he suffered, and being made perfect, he became the source of eternal salvation to all who obey him' (Hebrews 5:8-9). The ordered-ways-then-strength pattern is fulfilled perfectly in the incarnate Son.
7: The rest of the acts of Jotham, all his wars and his ways, are written in the Book of the Kings of Israel and Judah. The historical record as pointing beyond itself. All the acts of the kings are written; but of Christ's acts: 'the world itself could not contain the books that would be written' (John 21:25). The historical record of the kings is finite; the record of the King of kings exceeds all recording.
8: He was 25 years old when he began to reign and he reigned 16 years in Jerusalem. The reign duration — the same introductory data as 2 Chronicles 27:1, a closing formula for the reign. Christ's 'reign duration' is not bounded: 'of his kingdom there will be no end' (Luke 1:33). The formulaic close that ends every king's account has no place in the account of the eternal King.
9: Jotham slept with his fathers and they buried him in the city of David; and Ahaz his son reigned in his place. The transition from the faithful son to the unfaithful son — the most dramatic reversal between adjacent reigns in Chronicles. Christ's kingdom does not pass to an unworthy successor; 'he holds his priesthood permanently, because he continues forever' (Hebrews 7:24). The succession problem that plagues the Davidic line is resolved in the permanent priest-king who never dies into his successor.
